candidates.direction_name: Restore the accepted PTX hot-band anchor and discard the failed 256x128 probe
ranking_notes: This is recommended because the current surface is too far off the accepted base to support another meaningful experiment.
The rich NCU deltas make the restore argument stronger, not weaker: registers improved but throughput and barrier behavior regressed badly.
Treat the restored PTX surface as a recovery checkpoint, not as a new win.
candidates.hypothesis: The 256x128 pivot probe achieved the intended register drop but still ran at 30.168576 ms because barrier and short-scoreboard stalls dominated. The correct next step is to restore the accepted PTX hot-band surface immediately so the loop can continue from a sane baseline instead of iterating on a +5.98 ms regression.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Recovery to the known PTX plateau before any further latency-hiding or barrier experiment is attempted
candidates.risk: Low. This is a bounded recovery revert back to the accepted PTX surface, not a new optimization hypothesis.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the change starts widening beyond a bounded restore or if it fails to return the hot band to the accepted PTX dispatch path.

target_hotspots.explanation: Registers improved, but the probe still regressed overall throughput.
Barrier stalls became the dominant issue on the probed surface.
Short-scoreboard stalls rose sharply versus the accepted PTX baseline.

expected_local_changes: Route the hot band back to the accepted PTX 128x128 microkernel.
Remove or park the compact 256x128 probe path from the active dispatch.
Keep the recovery diff narrow enough that the next round can remeasure a clean baseline.

guardrail_metrics.reason: The current probe is materially regressed and must recover before forward search resumes.
Barrier stalls jumped on the failed probe and should come back down after recovery.
Short-scoreboard stalls are part of the current local failure mode.

candidates.direction_name: Recover the PTX baseline, then trim live control state before retrying geometry
ranking_notes: This remains the strongest forward family once the baseline is restored.
The 256x128 probe makes the PTX family more informative because it showed that geometry alone does not solve the latency-hiding ceiling.
It ranks behind dir_01 because recovery is mandatory first.
candidates.hypothesis: The failed 256x128 probe showed that reducing registers without improving active warps is not enough. After recovery, the best forward branch is still the accepted PTX family, but with a new emphasis: reduce live control state and fragment lifetime there rather than chasing more export arithmetic or another geometry jump.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: occupancy_latency_hiding_issue on the accepted PTX hot-band path, with barrier as a secondary seam
candidates.risk: Medium. This stays on the accepted PTX surface, but it needs careful trimming to avoid replaying already closed PTX variants.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation reopens already-closed PTX copy-order variants or widens into a geometry change instead of a control-state trim.

target_hotspots.explanation: Active warps stayed flat even after the register drop.
Registers are still constraining occupancy on the probed surface.
Barrier remains the clearest secondary seam to watch when the PTX baseline is restored.

expected_local_changes: Restore first, then keep the accepted PTX launch shape while trimming live control bookkeeping.
Prioritize steady-state fragment lifetime and control-state cleanup over another export-only tweak.
Use the 256x128 barrier regression as a guardrail, not as a reason to stay on the regressed surface.

guardrail_metrics.reason: Active warps must improve or at least stop regressing once the baseline is restored.
Barrier cost rose on the failed probe and should not be reintroduced.
Long scoreboard improved on the probe; the forward PTX follow-up should preserve that gain if possible.

candidates.direction_name: If 256x128 is revisited, pivot from register shaving to barrier and short-scoreboard cleanup
ranking_notes: This stays third only because the probe produced one useful new fact: the pivot can reach 167 registers.
It is far below the restore because a future revisit would need a very different local target than the one we just tried.
Do not select it again immediately unless the accepted PTX surface is already recovered.
candidates.hypothesis: The current probe proved that the 256x128 pivot can lower registers materially, but throughput still collapsed because the new local wall shifted to barrier and short-scoreboard stalls. If this family is revisited later, it should keep the 167-register floor and attack the 256x128 handoff / shared-fragment seam directly instead of doing another generic low-register transplant.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: synchronization_barrier_issue and short_scoreboard pressure inside the 256x128 pivot after the register budget has already been reduced
candidates.risk: High. This keeps a regressed family alive only because it produced a real register reduction, and any follow-up would still need to beat a large measured loss.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation does not directly target barrier or short-scoreboard behavior on the 256x128 path, or if it widens into another generic geometry replay.

target_hotspots.explanation: Barrier is the new dominant local stall on the 256x128 path.
Short-scoreboard rose sharply after the compact pivot probe.
The probe did achieve a meaningful register reduction that should be preserved if this family is revisited.

expected_local_changes: Leave writer ownership intact and preserve the lower register floor.
Attack the 256x128 wait-group, export, or shared-fragment seam directly instead of replaying another geometry-only shift.
Treat the current 30 ms run as a learning branch, not as a candidate surface to iterate on blindly.

guardrail_metrics.reason: Any future 256x128 salvage attempt must reduce barrier cost.
The next 256x128-specific step must also reduce the short-scoreboard wall.
A revisit should preserve the one successful register reduction from this probe.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
